questions.question: What does tenant isolation need to protect besides data confidentiality?
options: Only brand design and logo colors.
Performance fairness, operational blast radius, and control-plane safety in addition to data boundaries.
Nothing else, because authorization solves every multitenancy problem.
Only object storage bandwidth.
questions.explanation: Multitenancy failures include noisy neighbors, shared failures, and admin-plane mistakes.

questions.question: Why is one heavy tenant dangerous in a shared platform?
options: Because it can create skew, exhaust shared capacity, and degrade unrelated tenants.
Because premium customers never use shared systems.
Because storage systems cannot track tenant IDs.
Because queues do not support fairness.
questions.explanation: A single large tenant can distort load, latency, and cost across shared pools.

questions.question: Which design most directly reduces cross-tenant data leakage in shared caches?
options: Tenant-prefixing or otherwise scoping cache keys explicitly.
Increasing cache TTL for all keys.
Running more dashboards.
Storing cache values in larger JSON blobs.
questions.explanation: Explicit tenant scoping on keys is a basic but critical isolation control.

questions.question: Why are quotas and fair schedulers useful even when authorization is correct?
options: They protect performance isolation so one tenant cannot monopolize shared workers or throughput.
They replace the need for tenant IDs in storage.
They guarantee zero cost for premium tiers.
They eliminate the need for observability.
questions.explanation: Authorization protects data access, while quotas and fairness protect shared capacity.

questions.question: When is moving a tenant to dedicated capacity most justified?
options: When the tenant creates sustained skew, stricter compliance needs, or outsized blast-radius risk.
Whenever a tenant opens one support ticket.
Only when the fleet is completely idle.
Never, because shared infrastructure is always cheaper.
questions.explanation: Dedicated capacity is justified by isolation, fairness, or compliance requirements.

questions.question: Which metric best detects noisy-neighbor impact in a worker pool?
options: Per-tenant queue wait and concurrency share for top tenants.
Only the total number of deployed hosts.
Average markdown line length.
The age of TLS certificates.
questions.explanation: Tenant-scoped queueing and concurrency metrics reveal fairness breakdowns directly.

questions.question: What is the main risk of broad support tooling bypasses in a multitenant system?
options: They can quietly undermine tenant boundaries and expand blast radius during mistakes or incidents.
They improve fairness too much.
They reduce memory pressure in shared caches.
They prevent background jobs from running.
questions.explanation: Support or admin bypasses can bypass the very isolation model the product depends on.

questions.question: Why is shared-by-default plus selective carve-out often a strong answer?
options: It balances efficiency for the long tail with a path to isolate large or regulated tenants.
It guarantees no future migrations are needed.
It removes the need for quotas.
It only works for single-region systems.
questions.explanation: Selective carve-out preserves cost efficiency without pretending every tenant has identical needs.
